For a standard normal distribution, find:

P(z > c) = 0.4642

Find c rounded to four decimal places.

To find c, we need to find the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of 0.4642.

The standard normal distribution has a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. The cumulative probability P(z > c) is the area under the curve to the right of z = c.

However, since the standard normal distribution is symmetric, we can instead find the z-score corresponding to a cumulative probability of 1 - 0.4642 = 0.5358. This z-score represents the area under the curve to the left of z = c.

Using a z-table or calculator, we can look up the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of 0.5358. This z-score is approximately 0.1023.

Therefore, c ≈ -0.1023 (rounded to four decimal places).

For a standard normal distribution, find:

P(z > c) = 0.868

Find c rounded to four decimal places.

To find c, we need to find the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of 0.868.

The standard normal distribution has a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. The cumulative probability P(z > c) is the area under the curve to the right of z = c.

Therefore, we need to find the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of 1 - 0.868 = 0.132.

Using a z-table or calculator, we can look up the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of 0.132. This z-score is approximately -1.13.

Therefore, c ≈ -1.13 (rounded to four decimal places).

A company has a policy of retiring company cars; this policy looks at number of miles driven, purpose of trips, style of car and other features. The distribution of the number of months in service for the fleet of cars is bell-shaped and has a mean of 40 months and a standard deviation of 5 months. Using the 68-95-99.7 rule, what is the approximate percentage of cars that remain in service between 30 and 35 months?

To use the 68-95-99.7 rule, we can assume that the distribution of the number of months in service for the fleet of cars follows a normal distribution.

According to this rule:
- Approximately 68% of the data falls within one standard deviation of the mean.
- Approximately 95% of the data falls within two standard deviations of the mean.
- Approximately 99.7% of the data falls within three standard deviations of the mean.

Given that the mean is 40 months and the standard deviation is 5 months:
- One standard deviation below the mean is 40 - 5 = 35 months.
- Two standard deviations below the mean is 40 - 2(5) = 30 months.

Since we are interested in the percentage of cars that remain in service between 30 and 35 months, we are looking for the percentage of data within one standard deviation below the mean.

Approximately 68% of the cars will remain in service between 30 and 35 months.

The Acme Company manufactures widgets. The distribution of widget weights is bell-shaped. The widget weights have a mean of 56 ounces and a standard deviation of 9 ounces.

Use the Standard Deviation Rule, also known as the Empirical Rule.

Suggestion: sketch the distribution in order to answer these questions.

a) 68% of the widget weights lie between ______
 and _____


b) What percentage of the widget weights lie between 29 and 65 ounces? 
 _______%

c) What percentage of the widget weights lie above 38 ? 
_______ %

To use the Standard Deviation Rule (Empirical Rule), we assume that the distribution of widget weights follows a normal distribution.

a) According to the Empirical Rule:
- Approximately 68% of the data falls within one standard deviation of the mean.
- Therefore, 68% of the widget weights lie between (mean - 1 standard deviation) and (mean + 1 standard deviation).

Using the given mean of 56 ounces and standard deviation of 9 ounces:
- (mean - 1 standard deviation) = 56 - 9 = 47 ounces
- (mean + 1 standard deviation) = 56 + 9 = 65 ounces

So, 68% of the widget weights lie between 47 and 65 ounces.

b) To find the percentage of widget weights between 29 and 65 ounces, we need to calculate the area under the curve between these two values.

To find the z-scores corresponding to 29 and 65 ounces, we use the formula:
z = (x - mean) / standard deviation

For 29 ounces:
z = (29 - 56) / 9 = -3

For 65 ounces:
z = (65 - 56) / 9 = 1

Using a standard normal distribution table or calculator, we can find the cumulative probabilities corresponding to z-scores of -3 and 1.

The area under the curve between these two z-scores represents the percentage of widget weights between 29 and 65 ounces.

Approximately 81.85% of the widget weights lie between 29 and 65 ounces.

c) To find the percentage of widget weights above 38 ounces, we need to calculate the area under the curve to the right of 38 ounces.

Using the z-score formula:
z = (x - mean) / standard deviation
z = (38 - 56) / 9 ≈ -2

Using the standard normal distribution table or calculator, we can find the cumulative probability corresponding to a z-score of -2.

The area under the curve to the right of this z-score represents the percentage of widget weights above 38 ounces.

Approximately 97.72% of the widget weights lie above 38 ounces.

The Acme Company manufactures widgets. The distribution of widget weights is bell-shaped. The widget weights have a mean of 37 ounces and a standard deviation of 10 ounces.

Use the Standard Deviation Rule, also known as the Empirical Rule.

Suggestion: sketch the distribution in order to answer these questions.

a) 68% of the widget weights lie between 
_____ and ______


b) What percentage of the widget weights lie between 17 and 47 ounces? 
 _____%

c) What percentage of the widget weights lie above 7 ? 
_____ %

To use the Standard Deviation Rule (Empirical Rule), we assume that the distribution of widget weights follows a normal distribution.

a) According to the Empirical Rule:
- Approximately 68% of the data falls within one standard deviation of the mean.
- Therefore, 68% of the widget weights lie between (mean - 1 standard deviation) and (mean + 1 standard deviation).

Using the given mean of 37 ounces and standard deviation of 10 ounces:
- (mean - 1 standard deviation) = 37 - 10 = 27 ounces
- (mean + 1 standard deviation) = 37 + 10 = 47 ounces

So, 68% of the widget weights lie between 27 and 47 ounces.

b) To find the percentage of widget weights between 17 and 47 ounces, we need to calculate the area under the curve between these two values.

To find the z-scores corresponding to 17 and 47 ounces, we use the formula:
z = (x - mean) / standard deviation

For 17 ounces:
z = (17 - 37) / 10 = -2

For 47 ounces:
z = (47 - 37) / 10 = 1

Using a standard normal distribution table or calculator, we can find the cumulative probabilities corresponding to z-scores of -2 and 1.

The area under the curve between these two z-scores represents the percentage of widget weights between 17 and 47 ounces.

Approximately 81.85% of the widget weights lie between 17 and 47 ounces.

c) To find the percentage of widget weights above 7 ounces, we need to calculate the area under the curve to the right of 7 ounces.

Using the z-score formula:
z = (x - mean) / standard deviation
z = (7 - 37) / 10 = -3

Using the standard normal distribution table or calculator, we can find the cumulative probability corresponding to a z-score of -3.

The area under the curve to the right of this z-score represents the percentage of widget weights above 7 ounces.

Approximately 99.73% of the widget weights lie above 7 ounces.

For a standard normal distribution, find:

P(z > -1.31)
Express the probability as a decimal rounded to 4 decimal places.

To find P(z > -1.31) for a standard normal distribution, we need to calculate the area under the curve to the right of z = -1.31.

Using a standard normal distribution table or calculator, we can find the cumulative probability corresponding to a z-score of -1.31.

P(z > -1.31) ≈ 0.9049

So, the probability is approximately 0.9049 rounded to 4 decimal places.

For a standard normal distribution, find:

P(z < c) = 0.8475

Find c rounded to four decimal places.

To find c, we need to find the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of P(z < c) = 0.8475.

Using a standard normal distribution table or calculator, we can find the z-score that corresponds to a cumulative probability of 0.8475. 

The z-score is approximately 1.04.

Therefore, c ≈ 1.04 (rounded to four decimal places).
